pages.text: # ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods ## Course Syllabus — Draft v1 ### [Semester TBD] | University of Colorado Boulder --- ## Contact Information **Instructor:** Bobby Hodgkinson **Email:** hodgkinr@colorado.edu **Office Location:** AERO 150D **Office Hours:** By request — use the scheduling link provided on Canvas. **Response time:** Emails will be answered within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. --- ## Instructor Bio Bobby Hodgkinson — Associate Teaching Professor, Smead Aerospace Engineering Sciences Hi everyone. I'm Bobby Hodgkinson, and I'll be your instructor for this course. My background is in vehicle controls, with experience working on aerial unmanned systems (UAS) and underwater vehicles, where I've tackled challenges in dynamic modeling, system stability, automation, sensors, and actuators. I'm passionate about applying dynamics and controls principles to real-world aerospace problems and helping students bridge the gap between theory and practice — which is exactly what this course is designed to do. Outside of engineering, I'm interested in the intersection of AI and education. I serve on the executive board of the Rocky Mountain AI Interest Group, a regional non-profit focused on exploring and promoting the ethical use of AI in our community. ASEN 3501 will engage with AI directly — not as a subject of study, but as a professional reality that shapes how I've designed the course, the assessments, and the expectations for your work. --- ## About the Course ### Course Description ASEN 3501 develops the skills required to design, execute, and critically evaluate aerospace experiments with engineering discipline. The overarching goal is to train test engineers — professionals who design tests, characterize systems, and validate models for engineering organizations. The course is organized around a single question that opens Week 1 and closes Week 15: *How do you know this prediction is right?* Students work through three progressively demanding segments — executing experiments they are given (E1: Weeks 1–5), predicting before measuring in complex aerospace systems (E2: Weeks 6–10), and designing their own experiments from scratch (E3: Weeks 11–15) — with the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework (AMVF) as the organizing structure throughout. By the end of the course, students are not just able to run an experiment and report a number; they can justify belief in a result, quantify what they don't know, and design experiments that support credible engineering decisions. This is the physical modeling branch of the curriculum — the connection between mathematical models and the physical world. ### Course Learning Outcomes By the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. **Design and conduct** aerospace experiments by applying measurement fundamentals, instrumentation principles, and best practices in small team environments. 2. **Apply** statistical methods and uncertainty analysis to quantify experimental error and establish confidence in results. 3. **Integrate** theoretical models with experimental data to validate and evaluate aerospace system behavior. 4. **Implement** signal conditioning, sampling, and data acquisition techniques to ensure accurate experimental measurements. 5. **Communicate** experimental results effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and limitations. 6. **Identify, evaluate, and create** experimental methods or instrumentation beyond those provided by the instructor to foster independent professional growth. ### Prerequisites Completion of [ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, or introductory aerospace engineering — TBD by instructor] or instructor approval. Students are expected to have prior exposure to MATLAB and to the fundamental mathematics required to interpret physical models (calculus, basic linear algebra, introductory physics). No prior laboratory methods experience is required. ### Required Materials - **No required textbook.** All readings and reference materials are provided through Canvas. - **Primary reference standard:** ASME V&V 10-2006, *Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Solid Mechanics* — available through CU Boulder libraries. This document is a required reference from Week 1. - **Supplementary reference:** Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition — available through CU libraries and on reserve. Specific chapters assigned as the course progresses. - **MATLAB** is the primary required computing platform for all labs and assignments. Available at no cost through CU Boulder's license — installation instructions on Canvas. Students who prefer to use Python (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) or another platform may do so, but **the teaching team will prioritize assistance for MATLAB first and foremost**. Students using other platforms are responsible for translating any provided MATLAB code, functions, or toolbox references on their own. No special accommodation will be made for non-MATLAB workflows during lab sessions or office hours. - **GitHub account** (free) — required for course documentation and portfolio. A tutorial is provided in Week 1. Students are not expected to have prior GitHub experience. - **Project supplies:** Students may choose to purchase supplies for project activities (no required purchase). The approach is similar to first-year projects — if you want to run your chosen experiment with your own materials, that is encouraged but never required. Students are encouraged to **keep any components they acquire** during course activities — sensors, microcontrollers, breadboards, or other hardware — as these may be useful in future senior design projects. --- ## Communication Canvas is the primary channel for course-wide announcements. Enable your Canvas notifications so you do not miss time-sensitive information. Email is appropriate for individual coordination, emergencies, and course-related concerns that should not be discussed publicly. Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. Responses will be provided within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). If you have not received a response within 48 hours, a follow-up email is appropriate. In the event of a medical or family emergency, contact the Office of Student Support and Case Management at https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm. They will help coordinate across all of your courses and connect you with campus resources. --- ## Method of Instruction ASEN 3501 is an in-person laboratory course. Sessions include lectures, hands-on lab activities, in-class discussions, oral assessments, and peer review exercises. The course is organized into three segments of five weeks each: - **E1 (Weeks 1–5):** Phase I — Execution & Quantification - **E2 (Weeks 6–10):** Phase II — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation - **E3 (Weeks 11–15):** Phase III — Experimental Design & V&V Planning Some assigned content — readings, reference standards, AI literacy viewing, and tutorial materials — will be completed outside of class to maximize in-person time for labs, discussions, and oral assessments. Canvas is the platform for all materials, assignment submission, and course communication. Attendance is expected. In-person sessions are the primary learning environment. Oral assessments, comparative analysis exercises, and peer evaluations all happen in class and cannot be replicated outside of class. --- ## Student Responsibilities and Expectations ### Credit Hour Expectations ASEN 3501 is a 3-credit course. Per CU Boulder's credit hour policy, 3 credits corresponds to approximately **9 hours of total student effort per week** — 3 hours in class plus 6 hours of out-of-class work (reading, lab preparation, assignment completion, and portfolio documentation). Each assignment and lab document will include an estimated time to complete to help you plan accordingly. Students who are consistently exceeding these estimates should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference materials, and any pre-class viewings before each session. Lab documents will be posted in advance — you are expected to have read them before the lab begins. Arriving unprepared to a lab session reduces your own learning and slows your team. ### Engagement and Attendance Active participation is expected in every session. This is not a course where you can recover missed content by watching a recording — lab activities, oral assessments, and comparative analysis exercises are conducted in person. Attendance is not formally taken, but participation is monitored and is a graded component. Missing a session may result in a zero for any in-class assessment that was conducted. ### Timesheet Requirement Weekly timesheets are required throughout the semester. These are a brief record of how you spent your time on course activities each week. The purpose is professional habit formation — tracking your own effort is a skill that matters in every engineering job — not surveillance. Format and submission details will be provided on Canvas. *(Details TBD — see review table.)* ### GitHub Portfolio Students will use GitHub to document and publish work throughout the course. A lightweight tutorial is provided in Week 1. By the end of the course, you will have a documented portfolio of your experimental methods work — something you can share with future employers. This is a professional skill that has immediate practical value. --- ## Course and University Policies ### Generative AI (AI Usage Policy) This policy applies to ASEN 3501. It is not a department or university policy. **The short version:** AI usage is expected. The course is designed around that assumption. The assessments are designed to reveal real understanding regardless of how content was produced. **The full version:** We are at a point in engineering practice where AI tools can generate a lab report, a data analysis, a literature review, or a slide deck in a fraction of the time it would have taken a person. That is not a problem to resist — it is a fact to prepare for. The workload expectations in this course assume that you are using AI augmentation in your work. If you are not using it, you should be. Knowing how to work effectively with AI tools is part of what it means to be a well-prepared engineer right now, and that bar will only rise. Here is what follows from that premise: **Creation is cheap.** AI can produce a technically plausible lab report with appropriately structured sections, populated figures, and reasonable-sounding conclusions. That means the ability to *produce* a report is no longer an adequate demonstration of understanding. What AI cannot do — at least not without a human who deeply understands the domain — is tell you *what is right, what is wrong, what is missing, and how to make it better.* That critical evaluation skill is what this course is designed to develop. It is also what engineering employers are increasingly paying for. **The primary assessment mechanism in this course is critical evaluation of engineering content** — not the production of engineering content. You will frequently be given a report, a data analysis, or a presentation — produced by AI, by me, by a peer, or by a prior student — and your grade will depend on the quality of your technical critique. What is correct and well-supported? What is incorrect or unsupported? What is missing? What specific change would most improve it? Answering these questions well requires real understanding that cannot be faked. **Authentic assessments counterbalance AI use in three ways:** 1. **In-person oral assessments.** You will explain your work, your choices, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team — in person, without notes, without preparation time. You cannot delegate this to AI. Everyone who passes this course will have clearly articulated an understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. 2. **In-class comparative analysis exercises.** These are closed-book, in-person exercises where you evaluate provided content. You cannot use external tools. Your grade depends on the technical accuracy and depth of your critique. 3. **The E3 oral defense.** You will defend your own experimental design to a technical audience and respond to direct questions about every decision you made. **What this is not:** This is not a policy against AI, and it is not designed to catch or punish AI use. It is a policy that acknowledges the professional reality you are entering and prepares you for it. Using AI to help draft, analyze, refine, or audit your work is not just permitted — it is encouraged. The course even includes an early viewing assignment on AI literacy. The expectation is that you use these tools intelligently and that you take responsibility for everything you submit. **Transparency:** If AI tools substantially contributed to a submission, note it briefly at the end. Describe what tool you used and what you used it for. This is not a disclosure requirement designed to penalize you — it is professional practice. **What is not acceptable:** - Submitting AI-generated content as your own without engagement or modification - Using AI to fabricate data, measurements, or results - Using any external tool during closed-book, in-class assessments Students with concerns about this approach are welcome to discuss them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you for tomorrow, not to argue about whether tomorrow is coming. *The instructional team may use AI tools to aid in feedback generation and to gain insight into student progress. We will never use AI to produce numerical scores on summative assignments. AI-augmented feedback will always be reviewed and revised by a human before it reaches you.* ### Lab Environment Policy Safety is the first priority in every lab session. Anyone violating safe conduct may receive a zero for that lab and may lose access to lab facilities. Use of lab facilities is a privilege. Failure to wear required safety gear will result in a 10% grade penalty per infraction. Endangering yourself, others, or lab equipment is not acceptable under any circumstances. Professional behavior and respectful communication are expected at all times. Eating and drinking inside the laboratory is prohibited. ### Lecture Recording Notice Lectures may be recorded for educational purposes. Students who do not wish to appear in recordings must notify the instructor at least 72 hours in advance. --- ## Assignments and Grading ### Submission Policies **Platform:** Assignments must be submitted via Canvas unless otherwise stated. Submissions must include the names of all contributing members. **Individuals whose name does not appear on a group submission will not receive credit for the assignment. Groups are encouraged to leave off the names of individuals who did not contribute to the lab assignment.** **File format:** Follow the file format requirements specified in each assignment. Submissions that do not follow the required format may not be accepted. **Late work:** Late submissions will not be accepted without prior approval or a documented emergency. Extension requests must be submitted at least 24 hours before the due date. **Regrade policy:** If you would like to submit a regrade request for any assignment, you must submit the request via email to the instructor within **2 weeks** of the graded assignment return date. All regrade requests will be reviewed and approved by a course instructor — not teaching assistants, teaching fellows, or lab assistants. a. The regrade request must clearly state the reason you are requesting the regrade and what you believe the correct grade to be. Note that disagreement on the established rubric allocation of points is not a valid reason for a regrade and will not be considered. b. The regrade request must include, in a single combined PDF: an introductory statement addressing the above, a PDF copy of the original submission with portions highlighted that pertain to the regrade request, and any additional supporting information. c. **Points can be added OR removed** based on correctness. If a mistake was made in grading and too few points were awarded, the regrade request may increase the final score; however, if the instructor finds a mistake was made in grading and too many points were awarded, the regrade request may lower the final score. d. Regrades submitted in the **final month** of the course will only be entertained if the regrade alters an individual's final letter grade. We reserve the right to make changes to the course schedule based on emerging circumstances. Advance notice will be provided through Canvas announcements. The current syllabus and schedule will always be posted on Canvas. ### Assessment Philosophy This course assesses two distinct capabilities: the ability to *produce* experimental engineering work, and the ability to *critically evaluate* it. Both are graded. In some assessments, the critique grade is weighted equally to or more heavily than the production grade. This is intentional. As AI tools become more capable of producing technically plausible content, the engineering skill that retains and increases in value is the ability to evaluate whether that content is correct, complete, and fit for purpose. The comparative analysis exercises in this course — where you evaluate provided reports, analyses, and presentations on technical merit — are not supplementary activities. They are the primary assessment mechanism. Every student who passes ASEN 3501 will have: - Produced structured experimental work across all three course segments - Critically evaluated provided content (AI-generated, instructor-selected, or peer-produced) in multiple in-class exercises - Articulated their understanding of key fundamentals in in-person oral assessments — multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways - Defended an original experimental design in a formal oral presentation (E3) Formative assessments are frequent and low-stakes. The purpose of formative feedback is to show you where you are relative to expectations and to help you develop, not to produce a grade. Take them seriously — students who engage with formative feedback consistently outperform those who treat it as overhead. The final third of the course (E3, Weeks 11–15) is driven by you. You choose your experimental design direction. You select your instrumentation. You define the validation logic. The instructor's role shifts from source of knowledge to critical reviewer. --- ### Grading Structure > ⚠️ **DRAFT — GRADING SCHEME NOT FINALIZED** ⚠️ > > The following grade tier structure is a working placeholder that reflects the instructor's grading philosophy and approach. It is modeled on the ASEN 1030 tiered grading system. The specific requirements, thresholds, and weights are subject to revision before the course launches. Do not treat these as final. > > **Gate-based passing structure (intended):** The final grading scheme will incorporate a gate-based structure where each major deliverable set must be passed before progressing. Students who fail to pass all gates by two weeks before the end of term will not be eligible to participate in the final A-level expo. The specific gate definitions, draft-feedback-revise cycle, and AI+peer feedback evaluation components will be specified in the individual assignment documents. See `grade_breakdown.md` for full concept description. > > **Assignment creation note:** All assignments should include: (a) a draft-feedback-revise cycle; (b) a peer feedback step with grading on feedback quality; (c) a comparison-to-AI-feedback step; (d) an expected time-to-complete estimate; (e) a pre-submission checklist including an AI audit step. This course uses a **tiered grading system** focused on demonstrated understanding, engagement, and professional discipline — not on accumulated points. The approach is designed to reflect real-world performance expectations: meeting the minimum standard keeps you in the game; effort beyond the minimum is recognized and rewarded. **Grading Philosophy:** A grade of 'A' represents superior/excellent performance. A grade of 'B' represents good/better-than-average performance. A grade of 'C' represents competent/average performance, which is consistent with CU Boulder grading policy. Intermediate grades (C+, B–, B+, A–) will be assigned based on partial completion of the next tier's requirements, at the instructor's discretion. Individual grades may be adjusted based on instructional team observations — exemplary performance may increase a grade; consistently poor engagement may decrease one. **Final Grade Tiers (Draft)** | Requirement | C | B | A | |-------------|---|---|---| | Pass all lab reports (all required sections, adequate technical execution) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Pass minimum oral assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | 75%+ on in-class comparative analysis exercises and formative assessments | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all weekly timesheets | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Complete all E1 and E2 written deliverables | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong comparative analysis performance (demonstrated critique quality above threshold) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Strong E3 design project (complete V&V plan, instrumentation justification, oral defense) | | ✓ | ✓ | | Exceptional oral assessment performance across the semester | | | ✓ | | Outstanding E3 project with genuine beyond-provided-hardware investigation (CLO 6) | | | ✓ | *Note: This grading structure is a draft placeholder. Final grading scheme to be determined by instructor before course launch.* **Points System (Draft — to be revised before course launch):** This course uses a points-based participation and performance system layered on top of the pass/fail gate structure above. Points are earned above the minimum threshold and determine final letter grade. The following describes the intended system — exact point values and letter grade thresholds will be finalized before the course starts. **Point-earning activities:** - **In-lecture participation:** Questions asked, answers given, and contributions to in-class exercises during lecture — awarded based on participation and correctness. - **Supplemental activity completions:** Reading optional materials, watching supplemental recordings, completing E0_supplemental exercises — awarded upon verified completion. - **AI comparative analysis work:** Completing AI-generated content comparisons, evaluating AI feedback quality in assignments — additional points above the standard assignment grade. - **MathWorks Academy completions:** Completing MathWorks training modules (MATLAB, AI, data analysis tracks) earns bonus points. - **Approved student proposals:** Students may propose a supplemental activity (find something interesting, connect it to the course, submit for approval) — if approved, points are awarded upon completion. - **Above-expectations performance:** The grading team may issue bonus points for assignment performance that goes meaningfully beyond expectations. **Scheduling priority:** Students with higher point totals receive priority access to sign-up slots for mid-tier E2 lab assignments and in-class lightning talk time slots. Sign-up windows open in tiers: top point holders first, then remaining students. **Grade thresholds (draft — to be finalized):** Passing all core assessments (gates) earns a C−. The number of points needed for B and A grades will be published before the semester begins. An A is achievable without AI tools but requires substantially higher activity volume (MathWorks Academy completions, multiple supplemental activities, consistent participation). An A with AI fluency requires demonstrably higher quality output in fewer total activities. **Note:** This system is a draft. Exact point values, grade thresholds, and proposal review process will be published in a course supplement before the first week of class. See `grade_breakdown.md` for the full concept description. --- ### Assessment Types **Lab Reports** Structured written deliverables produced after lab activities. Required sections are specified in each assignment and evolve in complexity across the three course segments — from the first structured lab report in E1 (Week 2) to the full V&V plan in E3 (Week 13). All lab reports require MATLAB figures with captions that explicitly state the analytical conclusion. MATLAB is the required analysis tool throughout. **Assignment creation note:** Each lab report should designate a portion of the grade to peer evaluation and a portion to AI-generated content comparative analysis — students will evaluate the quality of feedback from both a peer and from an AI tool, and will be graded on the quality of that evaluation. The rubric for each assignment should make these dimensions explicit. This applies across E1–E3 deliverables. **Oral Assessments** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you explain your work, your methods, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team. These assessments cannot be substituted or made up without a documented emergency — no exceptions without documented circumstances and prior coordination. Oral assessments are scheduled throughout the semester during lab sessions. They are not surprise evaluations — you will know when they are coming and what they will cover. The purpose is the same as the code interviews in ASEN 1030: to ensure that demonstrated understanding is real, not performed. Every student who passes this course will have articulated their understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. **In-Class Comparative Analysis Exercises** Closed-book, in-person assessments in which you evaluate a provided report, data analysis, or presentation. The content being evaluated may be AI-generated, instructor-produced, or drawn from prior course iterations or professional literature. Your grade depends on the technical quality of your critique: what is correct and well-supported, what is incorrect or unsupported, what is missing, and what specific improvement would most strengthen the work. These exercises occur at least once in each course segment (E1 Week 3, E2 Week 9, E3 Week 14). **Lightning Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class presentations (3–5 minutes) in which you present experimental results, predictions, or design decisions to the class. Graded on clarity, technical accuracy, and the quality of responses to peer questions. Students also evaluate each other's lightning talks using structured written forms. **Peer Evaluation** Structured written evaluation of peers' work — including design frames, presentations, and experimental reports. Graded on the quality of the critique: specificity, accuracy of technical assessment, tone, and actionability of improvement recommendations. A vague or uniformly positive peer evaluation will not receive full credit. The skill being graded is the same as in the comparative analysis exercises — the source of the work being evaluated (peer, AI, or professional) does not change the standard. **E3 Design Project** The capstone of the course, spanning Weeks 11–15. You design an original aerospace experiment from scratch: define a predictive model and hypothesis, select sensors and DAQ configuration, specify signal conditioning strategy, produce a MATLAB-based uncertainty analysis, write a full V&V plan, and defend your design in a formal oral presentation. The project is documented in your GitHub portfolio. **Optional:** You may record a brief video presentation of your experiment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible only to those with the URL). This is not a graded requirement — it is an opportunity to build a professional portfolio artifact that can be shared through the instructor's network if you choose. If recording presents a concern, notify the instructor and an alternative will be arranged. This is the E3 segment in its entirety — not a single assignment. --- ## Academic Integrity All students are subject to the CU Boulder Honor Code. Academic integrity requirements apply to all work submitted in this course, regardless of the tools used to produce it. AI usage is explicitly permitted and encouraged per the AI Usage Policy above. However, students are fully responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and completeness of everything they submit. Using AI to produce plausible content and submitting it without understanding or verification is a failure of academic integrity — not because of the AI, but because of the misrepresentation. Submitting AI-generated content without engagement, fabricating data or results, or failing to disclose substantial AI contributions where disclosure is required may result in academic penalties under the Honor Code. If you are unsure whether a specific use of AI is acceptable in a given context, ask the instructor before submitting. The standard is straightforward: would you be able to explain every claim, every number, and every decision in your submission if asked in person? --- ## Accommodations CU Boulder is committed to providing equitable access for students with disabilities. Students needing accommodations must provide their accommodation letter to the instructor as early in the semester as possible. As a departmental policy, students should expect to receive accommodations for timed assessments only if the instructor receives the accommodation letter at least **5 business days before the assessment**. Late accommodation requests cannot be guaranteed. Contact Disability Services at https://www.colorado.edu/disabilityservices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your success matters beyond this course. If you are experiencing challenges — academic, personal, or otherwise — please use these resources: - **Student Support and Case Management:** https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm — for emergencies, complex personal situations, and coordination across multiple courses. - **Counseling and Psychiatric Services (CAPS):** https://www.colorado.edu/counseling — for mental health support. - **Academic Advising:** Contact your department advisor for academic planning questions. - **Writing Center:** https://www.colorado.edu/writingcenter — for support with technical writing. If you are struggling in this course and are not sure what to do, email me. That is what I am here for. --- ## University Policies A complete copy of CU Boulder's university policies — including the Honor Code, Title IX, and non-discrimination policies — is available at https://www.colorado.edu/policies and through the CU Resources page on Canvas. --- ## Agent Notes - **Instructor bio:** Adapted from the 1030 syllabus. The core paragraph is preserved almost verbatim. The AI/non-profit mention is retained per task instruction. The second paragraph is lightly updated to frame AI engagement in the experimental methods context (assessment design) rather than the 1030 framing (course content supplementation). - **AI policy section:** Written at length per task and instructor notes emphasis. Draws directly from notes.txt (3/20 entry) and instructor_clarification_notes.md Sections 1 and 6. The policy is substantially more forward-leaning than the 1030 version, which was more focused on disclosure/transparency. The 3501 version frames AI as an assumed tool with authentic assessments as the counterbalance. The "creation is cheap" framing is from notes.txt verbatim. - **Grading structure:** Modeled on the 1030 tiered system (C/B/A requirements table). MATLAB trainings replaced with 3501-appropriate requirements. Marked clearly as DRAFT throughout. Specific thresholds and weights not specified — flagged for instructor review. - **Oral assessments:** Named "Oral Assessments" rather than "Code Interviews" (the 1030 term). The 1030 rationale paragraph for code interviews is adapted and reframed for experimental methods context. - **Assessment types:** The 1030 quizzes/labs/MATLAB trainings structure is entirely replaced with 3501-appropriate assessment types from the week-by-week blueprints. - **Prerequisites:** Placeholders used — instructor must supply specific course numbers. - **Timesheet details:** Approach confirmed in instructor notes (notes.txt). nd the physical world. ### Course Learning Outcomes By the end of this course, students will be able to: 1. **Design and conduct** aerospace experiments by applying measurement fundamentals, instrumentation principles, and best practices in small team environments. 2. **Apply** statistical methods and uncertainty analysis to quantify experimental error and establish confidence in results. 3. **Integrate** theoretical models with experimental data to validate and evaluate aerospace system behavior. 4. **Implement** signal conditioning, sampling, and data acquisition techniques to ensure accurate experimental measurements. 5. **Communicate** experimental results effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and limitations. 6.

effectively through technical reports and presentations, with clear description of methods, results, and limitations. 6. **Identify, evaluate, and create** experimental methods or instrumentation beyond those provided by the instructor to foster independent professional growth. ### Prerequisites Completion of [ASEN XXXX / statics, dynamics, or introductory aerospace engineering — TBD by instructor] or instructor approval. Students are expected to have prior exposure to MATLAB and to the fundamental mathematics required to interpret physical models (calculus, basic linear algebra, introductory physics). No prior laboratory methods experience is required. ### Required Materials - **No required textbook.** All readings and reference materials are provided through Canvas. - **Primary

ired Materials - **No required textbook.** All readings and reference materials are provided through Canvas. - **Primary reference standard:** ASME V&V 10-2006, *Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Solid Mechanics* — available through CU Boulder libraries. This document is a required reference from Week 1. - **Supplementary reference:** Coleman & Steele, *Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers*, 4th edition — available through CU libraries and on reserve. Specific chapters assigned as the course progresses. - **MATLAB** is the primary required computing platform for all labs and assignments. Available at no cost through CU Boulder's license — installation instructions on Canvas. Students who prefer to use Python (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) or

Boulder's license — installation instructions on Canvas. Students who prefer to use Python (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) or another platform may do so, but **the teaching team will prioritize assistance for MATLAB first and foremost**. Students using other platforms are responsible for translating any provided MATLAB code, functions, or toolbox references on their own. No special accommodation will be made for non-MATLAB workflows during lab sessions or office hours. - **GitHub account** (free) — required for course documentation and portfolio. A tutorial is provided in Week 1. Students are not expected to have prior GitHub experience. - **Project supplies:** Students may choose to purchase supplies for project activities (no required purchase). The approach is similar to first-year projects

hoose to purchase supplies for project activities (no required purchase). The approach is similar to first-year projects — if you want to run your chosen experiment with your own materials, that is encouraged but never required. Students are encouraged to **keep any components they acquire** during course activities — sensors, microcontrollers, breadboards, or other hardware — as these may be useful in future senior design projects. --- ## Communication Canvas is the primary channel for course-wide announcements. Enable your Canvas notifications so you do not miss time-sensitive information. Email is appropriate for individual coordination, emergencies, and course-related concerns that should not be discussed publicly. Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. Responses will be provided

ated concerns that should not be discussed publicly. Include "ASEN 3501" in the subject line. Responses will be provided within 48 hours during business hours (Monday–Friday, 8:00 am–5:00 pm). If you have not received a response within 48 hours, a follow-up email is appropriate. In the event of a medical or family emergency, contact the Office of Student Support and Case Management at https://www.colorado.edu/studentaffairs/sscm. They will help coordinate across all of your courses and connect you with campus resources. --- ## Method of Instruction ASEN 3501 is an in-person laboratory course. Sessions include lectures, hands-on lab activities, in-class discussions, oral assessments, and peer review exercises. The course is organized into three segments of five weeks each: - **E1 (Weeks

al assessments, and peer review exercises. The course is organized into three segments of five weeks each: - **E1 (Weeks 1–5):** Phase I — Execution & Quantification - **E2 (Weeks 6–10):** Phase II — Predictive Modeling & Comparative Validation - **E3 (Weeks 11–15):** Phase III — Experimental Design & V&V Planning Some assigned content — readings, reference standards, AI literacy viewing, and tutorial materials — will be completed outside of class to maximize in-person time for labs, discussions, and oral assessments. Canvas is the platform for all materials, assignment submission, and course communication. Attendance is expected. In-person sessions are the primary learning environment. Oral assessments, comparative analysis exercises, and peer evaluations all happen in class and cannot

rning environment. Oral assessments, comparative analysis exercises, and peer evaluations all happen in class and cannot be replicated outside of class. --- ## Student Responsibilities and Expectations ### Credit Hour Expectations ASEN 3501 is a 3-credit course. Per CU Boulder's credit hour policy, 3 credits corresponds to approximately **9 hours of total student effort per week** — 3 hours in class plus 6 hours of out-of-class work (reading, lab preparation, assignment completion, and portfolio documentation). Each assignment and lab document will include an estimated time to complete to help you plan accordingly. Students who are consistently exceeding these estimates should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference materials, and any pre-class viewings

should contact the instructor. ### Preparation Review assigned readings, reference materials, and any pre-class viewings before each session. Lab documents will be posted in advance — you are expected to have read them before the lab begins. Arriving unprepared to a lab session reduces your own learning and slows your team. ### Engagement and Attendance Active participation is expected in every session. This is not a course where you can recover missed content by watching a recording — lab activities, oral assessments, and comparative analysis exercises are conducted in person. Attendance is not formally taken, but participation is monitored and is a graded component. Missing a session may result in a zero for any in-class assessment that was conducted. ### Timesheet Requirement Weekly

Missing a session may result in a zero for any in-class assessment that was conducted. ### Timesheet Requirement Weekly timesheets are required throughout the semester. These are a brief record of how you spent your time on course activities each week. The purpose is professional habit formation — tracking your own effort is a skill that matters in every engineering job — not surveillance. Format and submission details will be provided on Canvas. *(Details TBD — see review table.)* ### GitHub Portfolio Students will use GitHub to document and publish work throughout the course. A lightweight tutorial is provided in Week 1. By the end of the course, you will have a documented portfolio of your experimental methods work — something you can share with future employers. This is a

have a documented portfolio of your experimental methods work — something you can share with future employers. This is a professional skill that has immediate practical value. --- ## Course and University Policies ### Generative AI (AI Usage Policy) This policy applies to ASEN 3501. It is not a department or university policy. **The short version:** AI usage is expected. The course is designed around that assumption. The assessments are designed to reveal real understanding regardless of how content was produced. **The full version:** We are at a point in engineering practice where AI tools can generate a lab report, a data analysis, a literature review, or a slide deck in a fraction of the time it would have taken a person. That is not a problem to resist — it is a fact to prepare for.

k in a fraction of the time it would have taken a person. That is not a problem to resist — it is a fact to prepare for. The workload expectations in this course assume that you are using AI augmentation in your work. If you are not using it, you should be. Knowing how to work effectively with AI tools is part of what it means to be a well-prepared engineer right now, and that bar will only rise. Here is what follows from that premise: **Creation is cheap.** AI can produce a technically plausible lab report with appropriately structured sections, populated figures, and reasonable-sounding conclusions. That means the ability to *produce* a report is no longer an adequate demonstration of understanding. What AI cannot do — at least not without a human who deeply understands the domain — is

demonstration of understanding. What AI cannot do — at least not without a human who deeply understands the domain — is tell you *what is right, what is wrong, what is missing, and how to make it better.* That critical evaluation skill is what this course is designed to develop. It is also what engineering employers are increasingly paying for. **The primary assessment mechanism in this course is critical evaluation of engineering content** — not the production of engineering content. You will frequently be given a report, a data analysis, or a presentation — produced by AI, by me, by a peer, or by a prior student — and your grade will depend on the quality of your technical critique. What is correct and well-supported? What is incorrect or unsupported? What is missing? What specific

technical critique. What is correct and well-supported? What is incorrect or unsupported? What is missing? What specific change would most improve it? Answering these questions well requires real understanding that cannot be faked. **Authentic assessments counterbalance AI use in three ways:** 1. **In-person oral assessments.** You will explain your work, your choices, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team — in person, without notes, without preparation time. You cannot delegate this to AI. Everyone who passes this course will have clearly articulated an understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. 2. **In-class comparative analysis exercises.** These are closed-book, in-person exercises where you evaluate provided content.

-class comparative analysis exercises.** These are closed-book, in-person exercises where you evaluate provided content. You cannot use external tools. Your grade depends on the technical accuracy and depth of your critique. 3. **The E3 oral defense.** You will defend your own experimental design to a technical audience and respond to direct questions about every decision you made. **What this is not:** This is not a policy against AI, and it is not designed to catch or punish AI use. It is a policy that acknowledges the professional reality you are entering and prepares you for it. Using AI to help draft, analyze, refine, or audit your work is not just permitted — it is encouraged. The course even includes an early viewing assignment on AI literacy. The expectation is that you use these

s encouraged. The course even includes an early viewing assignment on AI literacy. The expectation is that you use these tools intelligently and that you take responsibility for everything you submit. **Transparency:** If AI tools substantially contributed to a submission, note it briefly at the end. Describe what tool you used and what you used it for. This is not a disclosure requirement designed to penalize you — it is professional practice. **What is not acceptable:** - Submitting AI-generated content as your own without engagement or modification - Using AI to fabricate data, measurements, or results - Using any external tool during closed-book, in-class assessments Students with concerns about this approach are welcome to discuss them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you

s Students with concerns about this approach are welcome to discuss them with the instructor. The goal is to prepare you for tomorrow, not to argue about whether tomorrow is coming. *The instructional team may use AI tools to aid in feedback generation and to gain insight into student progress. We will never use AI to produce numerical scores on summative assignments. AI-augmented feedback will always be reviewed and revised by a human before it reaches you.* ### Lab Environment Policy Safety is the first priority in every lab session. Anyone violating safe conduct may receive a zero for that lab and may lose access to lab facilities. Use of lab facilities is a privilege. Failure to wear required safety gear will result in a 10% grade penalty per infraction. Endangering yourself, others,

e. Failure to wear required safety gear will result in a 10% grade penalty per infraction. Endangering yourself, others, or lab equipment is not acceptable under any circumstances. Professional behavior and respectful communication are expected at all times. Eating and drinking inside the laboratory is prohibited. ### Lecture Recording Notice Lectures may be recorded for educational purposes. Students who do not wish to appear in recordings must notify the instructor at least 72 hours in advance. --- ## Assignments and Grading ### Submission Policies **Platform:** Assignments must be submitted via Canvas unless otherwise stated. Submissions must include the names of all contributing members. **Individuals whose name does not appear on a group submission will not receive credit for the

all contributing members. **Individuals whose name does not appear on a group submission will not receive credit for the assignment. Groups are encouraged to leave off the names of individuals who did not contribute to the lab assignment.** **File format:** Follow the file format requirements specified in each assignment. Submissions that do not follow the required format may not be accepted. **Late work:** Late submissions will not be accepted without prior approval or a documented emergency. Extension requests must be submitted at least 24 hours before the due date. **Regrade policy:** If you would like to submit a regrade request for any assignment, you must submit the request via email to the instructor within **2 weeks** of the graded assignment return date. All regrade requests will

e request via email to the instructor within **2 weeks** of the graded assignment return date. All regrade requests will be reviewed and approved by a course instructor — not teaching assistants, teaching fellows, or lab assistants. a. The regrade request must clearly state the reason you are requesting the regrade and what you believe the correct grade to be. Note that disagreement on the established rubric allocation of points is not a valid reason for a regrade and will not be considered. b. The regrade request must include, in a single combined PDF: an introductory statement addressing the above, a PDF copy of the original submission with portions highlighted that pertain to the regrade request, and any additional supporting information. c. **Points can be added OR removed** based on

ertain to the regrade request, and any additional supporting information. c. **Points can be added OR removed** based on correctness. If a mistake was made in grading and too few points were awarded, the regrade request may increase the final score; however, if the instructor finds a mistake was made in grading and too many points were awarded, the regrade request may lower the final score. d. Regrades submitted in the **final month** of the course will only be entertained if the regrade alters an individual's final letter grade. We reserve the right to make changes to the course schedule based on emerging circumstances. Advance notice will be provided through Canvas announcements. The current syllabus and schedule will always be posted on Canvas. ### Assessment Philosophy This course

announcements. The current syllabus and schedule will always be posted on Canvas. ### Assessment Philosophy This course assesses two distinct capabilities: the ability to *produce* experimental engineering work, and the ability to *critically evaluate* it. Both are graded. In some assessments, the critique grade is weighted equally to or more heavily than the production grade. This is intentional. As AI tools become more capable of producing technically plausible content, the engineering skill that retains and increases in value is the ability to evaluate whether that content is correct, complete, and fit for purpose. The comparative analysis exercises in this course — where you evaluate provided reports, analyses, and presentations on technical merit — are not supplementary activities.

where you evaluate provided reports, analyses, and presentations on technical merit — are not supplementary activities. They are the primary assessment mechanism. Every student who passes ASEN 3501 will have: - Produced structured experimental work across all three course segments - Critically evaluated provided content (AI-generated, instructor-selected, or peer-produced) in multiple in-class exercises - Articulated their understanding of key fundamentals in in-person oral assessments — multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways - Defended an original experimental design in a formal oral presentation (E3) Formative assessments are frequent and low-stakes. The purpose of formative feedback is to show you where you are relative to expectations and to help you develop, not to

The purpose of formative feedback is to show you where you are relative to expectations and to help you develop, not to produce a grade. Take them seriously — students who engage with formative feedback consistently outperform those who treat it as overhead. The final third of the course (E3, Weeks 11–15) is driven by you. You choose your experimental design direction. You select your instrumentation. You define the validation logic. The instructor's role shifts from source of knowledge to critical reviewer. --- ### Grading Structure > ⚠️ **DRAFT — GRADING SCHEME NOT FINALIZED** ⚠️ > > The following grade tier structure is a working placeholder that reflects the instructor's grading philosophy and approach. It is modeled on the ASEN 1030 tiered grading system. The specific requirements,

ctor's grading philosophy and approach. It is modeled on the ASEN 1030 tiered grading system. The specific requirements, thresholds, and weights are subject to revision before the course launches. Do not treat these as final. > > **Gate-based passing structure (intended):** The final grading scheme will incorporate a gate-based structure where each major deliverable set must be passed before progressing. Students who fail to pass all gates by two weeks before the end of term will not be eligible to participate in the final A-level expo. The specific gate definitions, draft-feedback-revise cycle, and AI+peer feedback evaluation components will be specified in the individual assignment documents. See `grade_breakdown.md` for full concept description. > > **Assignment creation note:** All

idual assignment documents. See `grade_breakdown.md` for full concept description. > > **Assignment creation note:** All assignments should include: (a) a draft-feedback-revise cycle; (b) a peer feedback step with grading on feedback quality; (c) a comparison-to-AI-feedback step; (d) an expected time-to-complete estimate; (e) a pre-submission checklist including an AI audit step. This course uses a **tiered grading system** focused on demonstrated understanding, engagement, and professional discipline — not on accumulated points. The approach is designed to reflect real-world performance expectations: meeting the minimum standard keeps you in the game; effort beyond the minimum is recognized and rewarded. **Grading Philosophy:** A grade of 'A' represents superior/excellent performance. A

minimum is recognized and rewarded. **Grading Philosophy:** A grade of 'A' represents superior/excellent performance. A grade of 'B' represents good/better-than-average performance. A grade of 'C' represents competent/average performance, which is consistent with CU Boulder grading policy. Intermediate grades (C+, B–, B+, A–) will be assigned based on partial completion of the next tier's requirements, at the instructor's discretion. Individual grades may be adjusted based on instructional team observations — exemplary performance may increase a grade; consistently poor engagement may decrease one. **Final Grade Tiers (Draft)** | Requirement | C | B | A | |-------------|---|---|---| | Pass all lab reports (all required sections, adequate technical execution) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Pass minimum

d-hardware investigation (CLO 6) | | | ✓ | *Note: This grading structure is a draft placeholder. Final grading scheme to be determined by instructor before course launch.* **Points System (Draft — to be revised before course launch):** This course uses a points-based participation and performance system layered on top of the pass/fail gate structure above. Points are earned above the minimum threshold and determine final letter grade. The following describes the intended system — exact point values and letter grade thresholds will be finalized before the course starts. **Point-earning activities:** - **In-lecture participation:** Questions asked, answers given, and contributions to in-class exercises during lecture — awarded based on participation and correctness. - **Supplemental

nd contributions to in-class exercises during lecture — awarded based on participation and correctness. - **Supplemental activity completions:** Reading optional materials, watching supplemental recordings, completing E0_supplemental exercises — awarded upon verified completion. - **AI comparative analysis work:** Completing AI-generated content comparisons, evaluating AI feedback quality in assignments — additional points above the standard assignment grade. - **MathWorks Academy completions:** Completing MathWorks training modules (MATLAB, AI, data analysis tracks) earns bonus points. - **Approved student proposals:** Students may propose a supplemental activity (find something interesting, connect it to the course, submit for approval) — if approved, points are awarded upon completion.

something interesting, connect it to the course, submit for approval) — if approved, points are awarded upon completion. - **Above-expectations performance:** The grading team may issue bonus points for assignment performance that goes meaningfully beyond expectations. **Scheduling priority:** Students with higher point totals receive priority access to sign-up slots for mid-tier E2 lab assignments and in-class lightning talk time slots. Sign-up windows open in tiers: top point holders first, then remaining students. **Grade thresholds (draft — to be finalized):** Passing all core assessments (gates) earns a C−. The number of points needed for B and A grades will be published before the semester begins. An A is achievable without AI tools but requires substantially higher activity volume

lished before the semester begins. An A is achievable without AI tools but requires substantially higher activity volume (MathWorks Academy completions, multiple supplemental activities, consistent participation). An A with AI fluency requires demonstrably higher quality output in fewer total activities. **Note:** This system is a draft. Exact point values, grade thresholds, and proposal review process will be published in a course supplement before the first week of class. See `grade_breakdown.md` for the full concept description. --- ### Assessment Types **Lab Reports** Structured written deliverables produced after lab activities. Required sections are specified in each assignment and evolve in complexity across the three course segments — from the first structured lab report in E1

n each assignment and evolve in complexity across the three course segments — from the first structured lab report in E1 (Week 2) to the full V&V plan in E3 (Week 13). All lab reports require MATLAB figures with captions that explicitly state the analytical conclusion. MATLAB is the required analysis tool throughout. **Assignment creation note:** Each lab report should designate a portion of the grade to peer evaluation and a portion to AI-generated content comparative analysis — students will evaluate the quality of feedback from both a peer and from an AI tool, and will be graded on the quality of that evaluation. The rubric for each assignment should make these dimensions explicit. This applies across E1–E3 deliverables. **Oral Assessments** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you

nsions explicit. This applies across E1–E3 deliverables. **Oral Assessments** In-person, in-lab assessments in which you explain your work, your methods, and your reasoning to a member of the instructional team. These assessments cannot be substituted or made up without a documented emergency — no exceptions without documented circumstances and prior coordination. Oral assessments are scheduled throughout the semester during lab sessions. They are not surprise evaluations — you will know when they are coming and what they will cover. The purpose is the same as the code interviews in ASEN 1030: to ensure that demonstrated understanding is real, not performed. Every student who passes this course will have articulated their understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple

ent who passes this course will have articulated their understanding of the key fundamentals multiple times, to multiple people, in multiple ways. **In-Class Comparative Analysis Exercises** Closed-book, in-person assessments in which you evaluate a provided report, data analysis, or presentation. The content being evaluated may be AI-generated, instructor-produced, or drawn from prior course iterations or professional literature. Your grade depends on the technical quality of your critique: what is correct and well-supported, what is incorrect or unsupported, what is missing, and what specific improvement would most strengthen the work. These exercises occur at least once in each course segment (E1 Week 3, E2 Week 9, E3 Week 14). **Lightning Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class

nce in each course segment (E1 Week 3, E2 Week 9, E3 Week 14). **Lightning Talks and Peer Presentations** Short in-class presentations (3–5 minutes) in which you present experimental results, predictions, or design decisions to the class. Graded on clarity, technical accuracy, and the quality of responses to peer questions. Students also evaluate each other's lightning talks using structured written forms. **Peer Evaluation** Structured written evaluation of peers' work — including design frames, presentations, and experimental reports. Graded on the quality of the critique: specificity, accuracy of technical assessment, tone, and actionability of improvement recommendations. A vague or uniformly positive peer evaluation will not receive full credit. The skill being graded is the same as

tions. A vague or uniformly positive peer evaluation will not receive full credit. The skill being graded is the same as in the comparative analysis exercises — the source of the work being evaluated (peer, AI, or professional) does not change the standard. **E3 Design Project** The capstone of the course, spanning Weeks 11–15. You design an original aerospace experiment from scratch: define a predictive model and hypothesis, select sensors and DAQ configuration, specify signal conditioning strategy, produce a MATLAB-based uncertainty analysis, write a full V&V plan, and defend your design in a formal oral presentation. The project is documented in your GitHub portfolio. **Optional:** You may record a brief video presentation of your experiment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible

**Optional:** You may record a brief video presentation of your experiment (private YouTube link — unlisted, accessible only to those with the URL). This is not a graded requirement — it is an opportunity to build a professional portfolio artifact that can be shared through the instructor's network if you choose. If recording presents a concern, notify the instructor and an alternative will be arranged. This is the E3 segment in its entirety — not a single assignment. --- ## Academic Integrity All students are subject to the CU Boulder Honor Code. Academic integrity requirements apply to all work submitted in this course, regardless of the tools used to produce it. AI usage is explicitly permitted and encouraged per the AI Usage Policy above. However, students are fully responsible for

usage is explicitly permitted and encouraged per the AI Usage Policy above. However, students are fully responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and completeness of everything they submit. Using AI to produce plausible content and submitting it without understanding or verification is a failure of academic integrity — not because of the AI, but because of the misrepresentation. Submitting AI-generated content without engagement, fabricating data or results, or failing to disclose substantial AI contributions where disclosure is required may result in academic penalties under the Honor Code. If you are unsure whether a specific use of AI is acceptable in a given context, ask the instructor before submitting. The standard is straightforward: would you be able to explain every claim, every

, ask the instructor before submitting. The standard is straightforward: would you be able to explain every claim, every number, and every decision in your submission if asked in person? --- ## Accommodations CU Boulder is committed to providing equitable access for students with disabilities. Students needing accommodations must provide their accommodation letter to the instructor as early in the semester as possible. As a departmental policy, students should expect to receive accommodations for timed assessments only if the instructor receives the accommodation letter at least **5 business days before the assessment**. Late accommodation requests cannot be guaranteed. Contact Disability Services at https://www.colorado.edu/disabilityservices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your

Contact Disability Services at https://www.colorado.edu/disabilityservices for assistance. --- ## Campus Resources Your success matters beyond this course. E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Lecture L1 --- marp: true theme: 3501 class: default paginate: false style: | @import url('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.0/css/all.min.css'); --- # The Aerospace Modeling &... marp: true theme: 3501 class: default paginate: false style: | @import url('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.0/css/all.min.css'); The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 1 The Two Questions This Course Answers
Is this prediction right?
Simulations and models generate quantitative predictions Prediction without validation is a guess with better vocabulary Experiments check whether the prediction holds
Will this do what we want?
Physical testing confirms real-world system behavior Quantify how closely prediction and reality agree Know what to do when agreement fails
You Are Training to Be a Test Engineer
Analyst / Modeler
Generates predictions from mathematical and computational models.
Instrumentation
Designs sensor systems and measurement infrastructure.
Test Engineer
Reads a model, designs the test, quantifies confidence, produces defensible conclusions.
The AMVF — Two Branches, One Validation Goal Left branch: conceptual → mathematical → computational model → simulation outcomes Right branch: physical realization → measurement → data reduction → experimental outcomes Both branches originate at the same system or phenomenon of interest Validation occurs where both branches converge at quantitative comparison Neither branch is credible without the other Verification vs. Validation — Not the Same Thing
Verification
"Are we solving the equations correctly?" Internal consistency: does the code do what the math says? A perfectly verified simulation can still have completely invalid physics
Validation
"Are we solving the right equations?" External correspondence: does the model represent physical reality? This course lives almost entirely in validation territory
Outcomes vs. Results — A Distinction You Will Use Every Week
Raw Sensor Output
→
Data Reduction
→
Result
→
Outcome
Uncertainty Is Mandatory — Here Is Why Same measured value: 44.1 N·m; model predicts 42.3 N·m Outcome A: 44.1 ± 0.4 N·m — gap exceeds uncertainty, significant discrepancy Outcome B: 44.1 ± 2.8 N·m — gap within uncertainty, agreement is defensible Same number, completely different engineering conclusions Uncertainty is not a footnote — it is the argument (ASME V&V 10-2006) Where Phase I Sits in the AMVF Phase I (Weeks 1–5): right branch only — physical modeling and quantification Mathematical model is given; your job is to execute the right branch with discipline Your work spans: physical realization → measurement → data reduction → outcomes A carelessly executed experiment cannot validate a good mathematical model Phase II adds predictive modeling; Phase III adds full design ownership The AMVF Connects to Professional Engineering Standards
ASME V&V 10-2006
Guide for V&V in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer.
AIAA Standards
Aerospace measurement and reporting standards for experimental data.
ASME PTC
Measurement uncertainty standards for performance testing.
The Experimentally Informed Model The goal of an experiment is not to confirm the model — it is to learn from comparison Discrepancy is not failure; it reveals what physics the model is missing A disagreement tells you what the model needs to account for Most valuable output: an experimentally informed model, updated by data This concept becomes the central deliverable in Phase II and Phase III What the Next Five Weeks Look Like Week 1: Framework + Design — AMVF, hypothesis, experiment design deliverable Week 2: Uncertainty Tools — partial derivatives, Monte Carlo, confidence intervals Week 3: Quantitative Comparison — validation metrics, comparative analysis memo Week 4: Full Pipeline — hypothesis through AMVF annotation, largest E1 report Week 5: Cross-Prediction — predict an experiment you did not run; bridge to Phase II Closure The Aerospace Modeling and Validation Framework is not a diagram you memorize and move on from. It is the decision-making structure that a test engineer returns to every time they need to argue that a result is credible — when they design an experiment, when they argue for or against agreement between data and model, and when they plan a V&V strategy for a complex system. Today you have seen where this course sits in that structure, why verification and validation are different things, and why uncertainty is not optional but mandatory. Over the next fifteen weeks you will internalize all of it — not by memorizing the framework but by using it, week after week, to make defensible engineering claims about physical systems. Is this prediction right?
- Simulations and models generate quantitative predictions - Prediction without validation is a guess with better vocabulary - Experiments check whether the prediction holds
Will this do what we want?
- Physical testing confirms real-world system behavior - Quantify how closely prediction and reality agree - Know what to do when agreement fails
--- # You Are Training to Be a Test Engineer
Analyst / Modeler
Generates predictions from mathematical and computational models.
Instrumentation
Designs sensor systems and measurement infrastructure.
Test Engineer
Reads a model, designs the test, quantifies confidence, produces defensible conclusions.
--- # The AMVF — Two Branches, One Validation Goal - Left branch: conceptual → mathematical → computational model → simulation outcomes - Right branch: physical realization → measurement → data reduction → experimental outcomes - Both branches originate at the same system or phenomenon of interest - Validation occurs where both branches converge at quantitative comparison - Neither branch is credible without the other --- # Verification vs. Validation — Not the Same Thing
Verification
- "Are we solving the equations correctly?" - Internal consistency: does the code do what the math says? - A perfectly verified simulation can still have completely invalid physics
Validation
- "Are we solving the right equations?" - External correspondence: does the model represent physical reality? - This course lives almost entirely in validation territory
--- # Outcomes vs. Results — A Distinction You Will Use Every Week
Raw Sensor Output
→
Data Reduction
→
Result
→
Outcome

Guide for V&V in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer.

Aerospace measurement and reporting standards for experimental data.

It is the decision-making structure that a test engineer returns to every time they need to argue that a result is credible — when they design an experiment, when they argue for or against agreement between data and model, and when they plan a V&V strategy for a complex system. Today you have seen where this course sits in that structure, why verification and validation are different things, and why uncertainty is not optional but mandatory. Over the next fifteen weeks you will internalize all of it — not by memorizing the framework but by using it, week after week, to make defensible engineering claims about physical systems. That is the skill employers pay for. That is what we are building together. Welcome to Aerospace Experimental Methods. ---

E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Lecture L2 --- marp: true theme: 3501 class: default paginate: false style: | @import url('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.0/css/all.min.css'); --- # Measurement Fundamentals... marp: true theme: 3501 class: default paginate: false style: | @import url('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.0/css/all.min.css'); Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2 Measurement Is Model Realization Measurement is not passive data collection — it is model realization A sensor does not directly record physical truth — it produces an inference Transduction → signal conditioning → digitization → calibration: each step introduces error The voltage on your screen is a model of the physical quantity, realized through hardware Understanding this chain is prerequisite to knowing what your measurement tells you The Transducer Chain
Transducer Element
→
Signal Conditioning
→
A/D Conversion
→
Recorded Value
What We Measure vs. What We Infer
What the Sensor Reports
Voltage, resistance change, capacitance shift Directly recorded by the hardware at the transducer stage Accurate but not the quantity you care about
What We Care About
Force, temperature, pressure — the physical quantity Inferred via calibration equation built on physical assumptions Inference fails if any assumption breaks down
Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies Bias: systematic offset — consistent, directional, does not average away Sources: poor zeroing, bad reference junction, mechanical preload Precision: scatter of repeated measurements around the mean Low precision is reduced by more measurements, better isolation, tighter control Knowing which problem you have determines the solution Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source Conduction error: sensor body conducts heat away from the measurement point Mounting stress: over-tightening alters mechanical boundary conditions at the gauge Electromagnetic interference: signal wires near power leads induce noise Installation errors often exceed the sensor's published specification error These are design choices made before data collection, not random events after Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not Calibration compares sensor output to a known reference and derives a correction equation Removes or reduces systematic bias across the measurement range A best-fit equation: Output = a·Input + b, applied to convert raw readings Calibration ≠ validation: calibration improves measurement fidelity Validation tests whether your model accurately predicts system behavior — distinct claim Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference A confounder causes the dependent variable to change for reasons other than your IV Can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent effects unrelated to the study variable Airflow changes between trials, temperature drift altering material modulus — both confound Antidote: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order Identify at least one confounder in your design deliverable before any data collection Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary
Hypothesis
Specific, falsifiable, causal: if [IV], then [DV], because [mechanism].
Variables
Independent, dependent, and controlled variables defined explicitly.
Confounders
Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented.
Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison
Weak Hypothesis
"If we change the input voltage, the measured power will change." Direction unspecified; no physical reasoning stated Not falsifiable in a useful sense — any change confirms it
Strong Hypothesis
"If voltage increases from 3V to 9V at fixed R, power increases by a factor of nine (P = V²/R)." Quantitative prediction with physical mechanism stated Clearly falsifiable — a threefold increase would be a discrepancy to explain

Transducer Element
→
Signal Conditioning
→
A/D Conversion
→
Recorded Value
--- # What We Measure vs. What We Infer
What the Sensor Reports
- Voltage, resistance change, capacitance shift - Directly recorded by the hardware at the transducer stage - Accurate but not the quantity you care about
What We Care About
- Force, temperature, pressure — the physical quantity - Inferred via calibration equation built on physical assumptions - Inference fails if any assumption breaks down
--- # Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies - Bias: systematic offset — consistent, directional, does not average away - Sources: poor zeroing, bad reference junction, mechanical preload - Precision: scatter of repeated measurements around the mean - Low precision is reduced by more measurements, better isolation, tighter control - Knowing which problem you have determines the solution --- # Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source - Conduction error: sensor body conducts heat away from the measurement point - Mounting stress: over-tightening alters mechanical boundary conditions at the gauge - Electromagnetic interference: signal wires near power leads induce noise - Installation errors often exceed the sensor's published specification error - These are design choices made before data collection, not random events after --- # Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not - Calibration compares sensor output to a known reference and derives a correction equation - Removes or reduces systematic bias across the measurement range - A best-fit equation: Output = a·Input + b, applied to convert raw readings - Calibration ≠ validation: calibration improves measurement fidelity - Validation tests whether your model accurately predicts system behavior — distinct claim --- # Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference - A confounder causes the dependent variable to change for reasons other than your IV - Can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent effects unrelated to the study variable - Airflow changes between trials, temperature drift altering material modulus — both confound - Antidote: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order - Identify at least one confounder in your design deliverable before any data collection --- # Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary
Hypothesis
Specific, falsifiable, causal: if [IV], then [DV], because [mechanism].
Variables
Independent, dependent, and controlled variables defined explicitly.
Confounders
Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented.
--- # Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison
Weak Hypothesis
- "If we change the input voltage, the measured power will change." - Direction unspecified; no physical reasoning stated - Not falsifiable in a useful sense — any change confirms it
Strong Hypothesis
- "If voltage increases from 3V to 9V at fixed R, power increases by a factor of nine (P = V²/R)." - Quantitative prediction with physical mechanism stated - Clearly falsifiable — a threefold increase would be a discrepancy to explain
--- # Connecting Your Design to the AMVF - Hypothesis and procedure → Physical Realization node - Sensor selection, installation plan, calibration → Experimental Measurement node - MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation → Data Reduction node - Final reported values with uncertainty → Experimental Outcomes node - Comparison to the predictive model → Quantitative Comparison node --- # Closure You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin. ---
