pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R1 # AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ## ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about... AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about a system is only as credible as the framework they used to make it — and professional experimentalists have spent decades building that framework already. The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework introduced in Week 1's lecture is not an invention of this course. It is a synthesis of engineering practice that has been codified over decades in standards documents, measurement handbooks, and validation guides produced by professional organizations whose job is to decide how engineers should argue for results. This reading is a map to those resources — not a reading list you must complete now, but a set of pointers that will become required references as the course progresses. The most important of these documents is ASME V&V 10-2006 , the primary engineering standard anchoring the AMVF framework used throughout ASEN 3501. Published by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, V&V 10-2006 establishes the conceptual and procedural framework for verification and validation in computational engineering. It defines the terms — verification, validation, uncertainty, model adequacy — in the precise, technical sense that practicing engineers use. When the Week 1 lecture distinguished verification (did you solve the equations correctly?) from validation (are your equations the right ones for this problem?), those definitions trace directly to this document. When the AMVF flowchart separates mathematical modeling from physical modeling and places quantitative comparison at the intersection, that architecture reflects the V&V 10-2006 framework. This standard is the language primer for everything that follows in this course. <image description: A two-column diagram showing the V&V framework structure. On the left, a "Mathematical Model" branch descends through equation derivation and numerical solution, terminating at "Verification." On the right, a "Physical Model" branch descends through experiment design, data collection, and uncertainty quantification, terminating at "Experimental Outcomes." A horizontal bridge labeled "Validation" connects the two branches at the bottom, with a bidirectional arrow labeled "Quantitative Comparison" spanning the gap. The overall structure mirrors the AMVF flowchart. A label in the lower corner reads "ASME V&V 10-2006." Clean black-on-white engineering diagram style, minimal shading, clear label typography.> Beyond V&V 10-2006, practicing aerospace experimentalists draw on a cluster of related documents. The AIAA measurement standards — produced by the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics — address measurement methodology specific to aerospace applications: wind tunnel testing, flight measurements, and propulsion characterization. These standards specify how uncertainty should be treated in measurement-intensive aerospace environments and will surface explicitly when E2's more complex experimental setups are introduced. The key point is that measurement standards are not generic — different engineering domains have domain-specific standards that define acceptable practice, and knowing which standard applies to your experiment is part of experimental design. For uncertainty analysis specifically, the ASME PTC (Performance Test Codes) standards provide procedural requirements for measurement uncertainty in engineering performance testing. These standards require that every reported result carry explicit uncertainty bounds derived by methods consistent with the standard's prescribed framework. If you are ever asked whether your measurement result is credible in a professional or regulatory context, the answer lives in whether your uncertainty analysis conforms to a recognized standard. <image description: A layered pyramid diagram with three tiers. The top tier is labeled "Primary Standards" and shows ASME V&V 10-2006 and AIAA measurement standards as formal document icons. The middle tier is labeled "Reference Texts" and shows Coleman & Steele as a textbook icon alongside the ISO GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) as a second document icon. The bottom tier is labeled "Practitioner Tools" and shows icons for lab documentation, MATLAB, and data recording. Arrows point downward from each tier to the tier below, indicating that the upper layers define the rules that lower-layer practice must follow. Flat-design illustration style, CU Boulder gold and black color palette.> The reference that bridges all of these to classroom practice is Coleman & Steele , Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers (4th edition). This book is the accessible bridge between the formal standards and the methods you will use in this course — beginning in Week 2, when partial derivative error propagation and Monte Carlo simulation are introduced. Relevant chapters will be assigned as the course progresses; having the book as a reference from Week 1 means you can follow the lecture directly into its theoretical foundations whenever you want to go deeper. The professional implication is easy to miss on first encounter: when you apply the AMVF in a lab report, you are not following a course-specific convention — you are operating within the same framework that engineers at NASA, aerospace contractors, and national labs use when they are required to document that a test was conducted correctly. Standards documents are the mechanism by which that framework is enforced across organizations and over time. ASME V&V 10-2006 will be a required reference in E3 when you develop your own V&V plan. The AIAA and ASME PTC standards will surface in E2. Knowing they exist — and having a sense of what they contain — changes how you read your own results. The Takeaway: ASME V&V 10-2006 is the professional anchor for the framework this course builds on — not a textbook chapter, but the actual document that practicing engineers cite when arguing that a test was done right. From Week 1, you are working within that professional tradition, not just a course format.

pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R2 # GitHub Basics for Course Documentation ## Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened — and in professional aerospace p... GitHub Basics for Course Documentation Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened — and in professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work. Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence. GitHub is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control — a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you will use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history. <image description: A screenshot-style illustration of a GitHub repository page for a hypothetical student project called "ASEN3501-Portfolio." The repository shows a README.md file rendered at the top with a brief description: "ASEN 3501 experiment documentation — Weeks 1–15." Below the README, a file tree shows folders labeled "E1_week_1/", "E1_week_2/", and files including "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." The interface uses GitHub's actual visual style — white background, dark top navigation bar, file tree with icons — rendered as a clean flat illustration rather than a screenshot. A small commit message is visible below the file tree: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 experimental design and AMVF diagram.""> The mechanics are straightforward. A repository (repo) is a folder with version history — a project folder that remembers every saved version. A README.md is a text file written in Markdown — a lightweight formatting language — that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You will write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers ( # Title ), bold ( **term** ), and lists ( - item ) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three steps: stage (select which files to include in this snapshot), commit (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and push (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you will not need the full depth of version control — branches, pull requests, merge conflicts — but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you are explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now. <image description: A three-step horizontal workflow diagram. Step 1 is labeled "Stage" and shows a folder icon with two files highlighted — "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." Step 2 is labeled "Commit" with a text bubble showing the message: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 design: hypothesis, variables, AMVF diagram." Step 3 is labeled "Push" with an upward arrow pointing to a cloud icon labeled "GitHub repository." Arrows connect each step left to right. Flat-design icons, CU Boulder gold accent color on the step labels, black and white otherwise.> Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file — which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps — this is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate. One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for final products. A repository with one massive commit at the end of the semester tells no story. Commits made as work progresses — small, frequent, with clear messages — create a record that shows how your thinking developed. That process record is a professional artifact. Engineers and employers who look at your GitHub portfolio are reading your technical judgment over time, not just the last thing you submitted. The Takeaway: The Tier 1 Phase 1 pre-lab work from this week's design session is your first portfolio entry — commit it to your repository with a clear description, and begin the documentation habit that every subsequent lab deliverable will extend.

pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Assessment A1 # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** 2–5 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 **Structure... ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: 2–5 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ — you will be assigned a specific experiment for each phase — but the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students regardless of which experiment they are assigned. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It is the natural order. It is also incomplete. A test engineer — the professional identity this course is building toward — does not just analyze data. Before the experiment runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself — accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, the signal chain — and commit to a prediction. Then they run the experiment. Then they compare. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you cannot dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real, and you have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 is the moment when these two perspectives come together. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data. Now you compare, contrast, and synthesize. What do two different experiments tell you that one experiment alone cannot? The three-phase structure is not busywork. It is the actual workflow of a test engineer. Assignment Overview Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual) What you are assigned: One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment — predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo — submitted before data is released. When: Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You will be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment — they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk) — you and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. When: Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model. Before the lab session Do the following before you arrive at the lab: Read the experiment-specific lab document for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document. Write a hypothesis — a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]." Sketch an AMVF diagram for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect — you will refine this after the lab. Identify your independent and dependent variables. Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. These are not graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable — they are required inputs to your Phase 1 report. Students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab. During the lab session Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis. Record your measurements using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound). Use MATLAB for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately — when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence. Phase 1 report — required sections 1. Experimental design Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least one confounding factor identified and addressed. 2. AMVF annotation Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one) to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan. 3. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 4. Multi-sensor comparison Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold, and the conclusion numerically — not just visually. 5. Uncertainty propagation For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error sources. Show your work. 6. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion. Interpret any discrepancy — is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model form limitations, or both? 7. Experimentally informed model Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be updated or refined. This is not about accepting or rejecting the model — it is about using the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what physical reasoning supports that change. 8. MATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation framework from E1 W2. 9. Plain-language summary A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer. This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix. Phase 1 report — format and submission Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections. Format: Markdown or PDF submitted to Canvas. Figures embedded in the document. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you have never run — predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit it before you see any real data. What "reverse" means In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it. In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not just of the physical phenomenon. This requires you to think about: What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs? Where are the likely failure modes — what could produce data that looks anomalous? A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate whether an experiment is worth running, whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, and whether you will be able to detect anomalies in real time during the test. What you receive for Phase 2 You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment (same document the students who ran it received) The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments) A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab You do not receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet. Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections 1. Predicted system response What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features — for example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s over 5 seconds, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions. 2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet. Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result as: [quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units]. 3. Predicted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4. Dominant uncertainty sources List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution. Justify the ranking using your propagation results. 5. Acceptance criterion Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of your predicted value, you will consider the prediction confirmed. State this threshold before you see the data. 6. Risk assessment Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data — not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release date. Once submitted, your prediction is frozen. You will not be allowed to revise it after data is released. Grading philosophy for Phase 2 A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning and committed uncertainty analysis, scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This is not arbitrary — it is the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing. This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have questions about it, ask before you submit. Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions The pairing You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared. The presentation structure Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately 8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time). Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1) Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment — what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then: what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. chunks.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Assessment A1 # ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment ## Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Weeks:** 2–5 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 **Structure... ASEN 3501 — Tier 1 Lab Assignment Forward Analysis, Reverse Prediction, and Combined Synthesis Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Weeks: 2–5 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 3, CLO 5 Structure: Three phases — Individual → Individual → Paired (→ Group of 4) Assessment: Phase 1 report (individual) + Phase 2 prediction memo (individual) + Phase 3 combined presentation (paired or group of 4) What This Assignment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical

nment Is The Tier 1 assignment is the primary lab sequence of E1. Every student completes all three phases. The physical experiments differ — you will be assigned a specific experiment for each phase — but the analytical framework, the deliverables, and the expectations are identical for all students regardless of which experiment they are assigned. Read this document carefully before you touch any hardware. The structure is intentional, and understanding why each phase exists will make each one more productive. Why Three Phases? Most experimental courses ask you to run an experiment and analyze the data. That is Phase 1. It is the natural order. It is also incomplete. A test engineer — the professional identity this course is building toward — does not just analyze data. Before the

ete. A test engineer — the professional identity this course is building toward — does not just analyze data. Before the experiment runs, a test engineer asks: what should the data look like? They build a model of the experiment itself — accounting for the sensors, the data acquisition, the signal chain — and commit to a prediction. Then they run the experiment. Then they compare. This pre-commitment changes everything about how you interpret discrepancy. When you see a gap between your model and your data, and you committed to the model before you saw the data, you cannot dismiss the gap as a modeling choice made after the fact. The gap is real, and you have to explain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 is the moment when these two perspectives come together. You ran one experiment. Your

plain it. That is Phase 2. Phase 3 is the moment when these two perspectives come together. You ran one experiment. Your partner ran a different one. You each predicted the other's experiment before seeing the data. Now you compare, contrast, and synthesize. What do two different experiments tell you that one experiment alone cannot? The three-phase structure is not busywork. It is the actual workflow of a test engineer. Assignment Overview Phase 1 — Forward Analysis (Individual) What you are assigned: One experiment from the Tier 1 experiment pool. What you do: Execute the experiment, collect data, analyze uncertainty, compare to a predictive model, and propose at least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see

least one experimentally informed model improvement. What you produce: A Phase 1 Individual Report. When: Weeks 2–4 (see Canvas for your assigned experiment and specific due dates). Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction (Individual, Blind) What you are assigned: A different experiment from the Tier 1 pool — one you did not run in Phase 1. What you do: Given only the theory, the sensor specifications, and the DAQ setup description for this unfamiliar experiment — predict what the data should look like. You do not have access to the actual data. You do not touch the hardware. What you produce: A Phase 2 Prediction Memo — submitted before data is released. When: Week 4 (prediction submitted; data released after the due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you

on submitted; data released after the due date). Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis (Paired, with optional group of 4) Who you work with: You will be paired with a student who did the opposite assignment — they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1 and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. What you do: Compare predictions to real data across both experiments. Identify what the predictions got right, what they missed, and why. Synthesize across both experiments. What you produce: A combined oral presentation (lightning talk) — you and your partner present together. If logistics allow, your pair joins one other pair for a group of 4 presentation. When: Week 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The

eek 5 (see Canvas for presentation schedule and sign-up instructions). Phase 1 — Forward Analysis: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Run your assigned experiment, characterize the uncertainty in your measurements, compare your data to a predictive model, and use the data to improve that model. Before the lab session Do the following before you arrive at the lab: Read the experiment-specific lab document for your assigned experiment. This document (provided separately) contains the theory primer, sensor specifications, and equipment list for your experiment. It is not this document. Write a hypothesis — a one-sentence, falsifiable prediction of the experimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be

erimental outcome. A hypothesis is not "I think it will work." It is "I predict the measured value of [quantity] will be [value or range], because [brief physical reasoning]." Sketch an AMVF diagram for your experiment. Map each step of your planned procedure to a node in the Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework. You don't need to be perfect — you will refine this after the lab. Identify your independent and dependent variables. Identify at least one control condition and at least one potential confounding factor. These are not graded as a separate pre-lab deliverable — they are required inputs to your Phase 1 report. Students who arrive without them typically need significantly more time to complete the lab. During the lab session Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods

significantly more time to complete the lab. During the lab session Use at least two sensor types or measurement methods to measure the same physical quantity. This is required, not optional. The comparison between sensors is part of the analysis. Record your measurements using the correct format: value, units, uncertainty. Every measurement in your report must carry an uncertainty estimate with a stated basis (e.g., manufacturer spec, resolution, or statistical bound). Use MATLAB for data collection, processing, and plotting. MATLAB Mobile is available for phone-based data acquisition where applicable to your experiment. The experiment-specific lab document will indicate whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately —

e whether MATLAB Mobile is used. Note anomalies in real time. If your data looks unexpected, write it down immediately — when it happened, what changed, and what you observed. These notes are evidence. Phase 1 report — required sections 1. Experimental design Hypothesis (from pre-lab), independent and dependent variables, control conditions, at least one confounding factor identified and addressed. 2. AMVF annotation Your annotated AMVF diagram, mapping each step of the actual procedure (not the planned one) to a framework node. Note any steps that did not match your pre-lab plan. 3. Measurement record Your recorded values for all primary measurements, with units and uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 4. Multi-sensor comparison

d uncertainty for each. Describe the basis for each uncertainty estimate. Present in a table. 4. Multi-sensor comparison Side-by-side comparison of the measurements from your two sensor types or methods. Apply a named validation metric to determine whether they agree. State the metric, the threshold, and the conclusion numerically — not just visually. 5. Uncertainty propagation For the primary derived quantity in your experiment: apply first-order error propagation using partial derivatives to compute uncertainty. Identify and rank the dominant error sources. Show your work. 6. Model comparison Compare your experimental result (with uncertainty bounds) to your predictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion.

edictive model. Use a named validation metric. State whether the comparison meets your pre-defined acceptance criterion. Interpret any discrepancy — is it attributable to measurement uncertainty, model form limitations, or both? 7. Experimentally informed model Identify at least one way your experimental results suggest the predictive model should be updated or refined. This is not about accepting or rejecting the model — it is about using the data to make the model better. Describe specifically what you would change, and what physical reasoning supports that change. 8. MATLAB figures All plots must have: title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple datasets, uncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation

ncertainty bounds where applicable, and a caption that states the takeaway in one sentence. Apply the graph presentation framework from E1 W2. 9. Plain-language summary A 3–5 sentence summary of what you did and what your result means, written for a non-engineer. This section belongs in your introduction or conclusion, not as an appendix. Phase 1 report — format and submission Length: No page limit. There is no credit for length. The value is in the quality of argument and the completeness of required sections. Format: Markdown or PDF submitted to Canvas. Figures embedded in the document. Submission: See Canvas for due date. Phase 2 — Reverse Prediction: Full Instructions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you have

ions The assignment in one sentence Given only the theory, sensor specs, and DAQ configuration of an experiment you have never run — predict what the data will look like, commit to that prediction in writing, and submit it before you see any real data. What "reverse" means In Phase 1, you followed the natural order: build the experiment, collect data, analyze it. In Phase 2, the order is reversed. You start with the experimental setup on paper and work backwards to the expected output. You are building a model of the experiment itself, not just of the physical phenomenon. This requires you to think about: What physical quantity is being measured, and what is its expected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that

ected range given the physical setup? What is the uncertainty in each sensor measurement, given the specs? How does that uncertainty propagate to the derived quantity? What would a time-series or distribution plot of the output actually look like, given those inputs? Where are the likely failure modes — what could produce data that looks anomalous? A test engineer answers these questions before the test runs. This is how you evaluate whether an experiment is worth running, whether the sensors are capable of resolving the quantity of interest, and whether you will be able to detect anomalies in real time during the test. What you receive for Phase 2 You will receive a Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment (same document

Phase 2 Experiment Packet for your assigned experiment. It contains: The theory primer for the experiment (same document the students who ran it received) The sensor specifications (manufacturer datasheets or equivalent) The DAQ configuration (sample rate, resolution, channel assignments) A description of the physical setup and nominal operating conditions The hypothesis that the student who ran the experiment submitted in pre-lab You do not receive: the actual data, any plots, any results, or any lab report from the student who ran the experiment. Your prediction must be made entirely from the packet. Phase 2 prediction memo — required sections 1. Predicted system response What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features — for example: "I

esponse What do you expect the data to look like? Describe the expected trend, range, and key features — for example: "I expect the measured angular velocity to increase from 0 to approximately X rad/s over 5 seconds, with a decay phase beginning around t = 7 s due to friction." Commit to specific values and ranges. Vague predictions are not predictions. 2. Predicted uncertainty in the primary derived quantity Apply first-order error propagation using the sensor specifications from the packet. Compute u for the primary derived quantity. Show the partial derivatives. State the result as: [quantity] = [predicted value] ± [predicted uncertainty] [units]. 3. Predicted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the

ted plot sketch Sketch (by hand or in MATLAB) what you expect a plot of the primary output quantity vs. time (or vs. the independent variable) to look like, with uncertainty bounds included. Label all axes with units. This sketch is your committed prediction. 4. Dominant uncertainty sources List the top two or three sources of uncertainty in order of expected contribution. Justify the ranking using your propagation results. 5. Acceptance criterion Define a numerical acceptance criterion: if the actual data falls within [threshold] of your predicted value, you will consider the prediction confirmed. State this threshold before you see the data. 6. Risk assessment Identify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data — not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model

tify one or two scenarios that would produce anomalous data — not because of a faulty experiment, but because of a model limitation or an edge case in the physics. Describe what anomalous data would look like and what you would investigate first. Submission: The prediction memo is submitted through Canvas before the data release date. Once submitted, your prediction is frozen. You will not be allowed to revise it after data is released. Grading philosophy for Phase 2 A prediction that turns out to be wrong, but was derived from sound physical reasoning and committed uncertainty analysis, scores higher than a prediction that turns out to be right but was derived from vague intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This is not arbitrary —

e intuition. The grade is on the quality of your reasoning, not the accuracy of your prediction. This is not arbitrary — it is the actual professional standard. An engineer who commits to a wrong prediction for good reasons learns from the discrepancy. An engineer who gets lucky learns nothing. This philosophy will be stated explicitly in class before Phase 2 begins. If you have questions about it, ask before you submit. Phase 3 — Combined Synthesis: Presentation Instructions The pairing You will be paired with a student who has the complementary assignment set: they ran your Phase 2 experiment as their Phase 1, and predicted your Phase 1 experiment as their Phase 2. This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have

This means: You have the Phase 1 data for Experiment A Your partner has the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment A You have the Phase 2 prediction for Experiment B Your partner has the Phase 1 data for Experiment B Phase 3 is the moment when these are compared. The presentation structure Your paired presentation has three segments, delivered jointly. Aim for approximately 8–10 minutes total (instructor will confirm against enrollment and available class time). Segment 1 — Forward (each student presents their own Phase 1) Each student in the pair briefly presents their own experiment: what they measured, what their result was, and what it revealed about the system. 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student

minutes per student. Segment 2 — Reverse (each student presents their prediction of the other's experiment) Each student presents their Phase 2 prediction for the other's experiment — what they expected, what the acceptance criterion was, and then: what the actual data showed. Did the prediction hold? Where did it diverge? Why? 2–3 minutes per student. Segment 3 — Synthesis (presented jointly) The pair synthesizes across both experiments. This is the most important segment and will likely be the most distinctive across presentations. What do these two experiments together tell you about the physical phenomena, the models, or the measurement approach that neither experiment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the

eriment alone could tell you? If you were designing a follow-on test, what would you change and why? 2–3 minutes for the pair. Group of 4 (if applicable) If resources and enrollment allow, your pair will be combined with another pair that worked on the same two experiments. The group of 4 presents jointly — each pair presents Segments 1 and 2 from their own perspective, and the full group of 4 delivers Segment 3 together. This creates a richer synthesis: four data points, four predictions, two experiments. The instructor will confirm whether group-of-4 presentations are scheduled for your section before Week 4. a written peer evaluation for each presentation. Your grade includes both your own presentation score and the quality of your peer evaluations. A strong peer evaluation: names a specific strength with evidence, names a specific weakness with evidence, and gives a concrete and actionable improvement recommendation.
